coredata.dc:title: Table sugar derived Carbon dot – a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Ansi, V.A
Renuka, N.K
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of carbon dots through an easy and economic synthetic strategy is reported herein, using table sugar as the precursor. The synthesis route adopted is entirely devoid of chemical modifications/post-chemical treatments. Visible aggregation of table sugar derived carbon dots in presence of Pb (II) ions leads to a novel method of identification of these toxic ions by naked eye. A simple turbidimeter could quantify the amount of Pb (II) in parts per billion (ppb) levels with extra ordinary selectivity. The limit of detection (LOD) achieved by this method is 14 ppb, which is highly remarkable in view of the permissible amount of Pb (II) ions in drinking water (15 ppb), as prescribed by U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).The facile synthesis of carbon dots as well as simple and fast Pb (II) sensing strategy with admirably low limit of detection are the highlights of the present work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Table sugar
Carbon dots
Aggregation
Pb2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots from table sugar (TS-CDs)
Plausible mechanism of formation of TS-CDs
Visible detection of Pb2+ ion in parts per billion (ppb) levels
Aggregation of TS-CD system- verification through characterisation results
Quantitative sensing of Pb2+ ion –Turbidimetric analysis
Validation of TS-CDs sensing efficiency using real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM image of carbon dots. (b)EDAX pattern with atomic percentage of elements. (c) AFM image of diluted carbon dot solution. (d) Height profile along the line in figure c.

Characteristics of C-dots (a) XRD pattern. (b) FTIR spectrum. (c) Raman spectrum of freeze-dried powder. (d) UV–vis absorbance spectra. (e) Photo Luminescence emission spectra of carbon dot solution (Exc. 350 nm).
Visual aggregation of TS-CD particles upon the addition of ppb amounts os Pb (II) solution. TS-CD solution before the addition of Pb (II) ions (left). After the addition of Pb (II) ions (right).

(a) Selective visual sensing of Pb2+ ion (All the test ions except Pb2+ ions produce negative response under similar conditions). (b) Carbon dot solution with mixture of metal ions (Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cr6+, Mn2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+ except Pb2+ ions) (left); after the addition of Pb2+ ions to this mixture (right).

Proof for aggregation of TS-CD system. (a)TEM image of the aggregated system. (b) EDAX pattern of aggregated system. (c) photoluminescence spectra of TS-CD solution and filtrate obtained after the removal of aggregated portion. (d)XRD pattern of aggregated TS-CD system.
FTIR spectrum showing direct involvement of carboxylate groups in the aggregation process.
Quantitative variation of turbidity of TS-CD solution with concentration of Pb2+ ion.
Time dependence study of carbon dot aggregation monitored using turbidimetry.
Comparison of reported sensors for the detection of Pb (II) ions.

row.entry: Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) derived carbon dot
Gold-Nano particles on the surface of graphene
Unmodified AuNPs and lead-stabilized G-quartet formation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recovery calculation for real-water samples using the prepared carbon dot solution.

head.ce:title: Table sugar derived Carbon dot – a naked eye sensor for toxic Pb2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Kerala, 673 635, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of carbon dots through an easy and economic synthetic strategy is reported herein, using table sugar as the precursor. The synthesis route adopted is entirely devoid of chemical modifications/post-chemical treatments. Visible aggregation of table sugar derived carbon dots in presence of Pb (II) ions leads to a novel method of identification of these toxic ions by naked eye. A simple turbidimeter could quantify the amount of Pb (II) in parts per billion (ppb) levels with extra ordinary selectivity. The limit of detection (LOD) achieved by this method is 14 ppb, which is highly remarkable in view of the permissible amount of Pb (II) ions in drinking water (15 ppb), as prescribed by U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).The facile synthesis of carbon dots as well as simple and fast Pb (II) sensing strategy with admirably low limit of detection are the highlights of the present work.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), one of the latest entries of carbon nano world, are tiny particles of carbon (less than 10 nm in size) with some sort of surface passivation. These particles were first discovered quite accidentally during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes [1]. The structure and components of CDs determine their diverse properties, which in turn decide their applications. Carbon Dots derived from different precursors exhibit different structures and find applications in various fields [2,3]. On account of the consummate features discovered in recent period, they are considered as amazing candidates among the nano carbon family. Many of the outstanding features of carbon dots were exploited in the fields of energy devices, environmental applications, bio-medical fields as well as in the fabrication of composite materials. The ability for electron storage and transport has been utilized in the designing of carbon nanodots based solar cells [4–6], organic LEDs [7,8], photo catalysts [9] and photo detectors [10]. Recently, they are also being explored for their applications as super capacitors [11].The most studied photoluminescence (PL) character outweighs all the other features, and moulds it as a promising green alternative to luminescent organic dyes and inorganic quantum dots in optics.

Carbon dots are conventionally synthesised from carbonaceous materials like graphite and carbon nanotubes by physical methods like laser ablation [12], vapour deposition of soot [13] microwave synthesis [14,15] ultrasonic treatment [16,17] plasma treatment, arc discharge [1], electrochemical oxidation, thermal oxidation and wet chemical/electrochemical methods [18–20].Green chemistry assisted fabrication of CDs has received recent attention, which eases the labour, reduces both the cost and time, and results in the formation of environmentally benign material with high yield. Recently, several groups have succeeded in the green synthesis of carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment from many abundantly available natural materials like banana juice [19] orange peel [20] sugar cane bagasse [21], sugar cane juice [22], trapabispinosa peel [23] etc.

Present work demonstrates an extremely facile and environment friendly fabrication of highly luminescent carbon nanodots from commercially available Table sugar (TS-CDs). Chandra et al. and Mehta et al. have proven that sucrose containing materials can serve as excellent precursors for highly luminescent carbon nanoparticles. The former group has attained luminescent carbon nano particles from sucrose [24]. However, the usage of ortho-phosphoric acid and stringent separation procedures make the synthesis route very complicated and un-welcoming. Mehta and co-workers have reported the fabrication of water dispersible carbon nanodots from Saccharum officinarum (sugar cane) juice which is excellent for bio-imaging application [25]. But, their synthetic strategy was quite tedious and it demanded the use of dichloromethane (DCM) during the purification stage. Time consuming post-chemical treatments including ultra centrifugation also can be raised as the demerit of this procedure.

Present work demonstrates a synthetic route which is entirely devoid of toxic materials as well as tedious post-chemical treatments such as ultra filtration, dialysis, centrifugation etc. The prepared carbon dots are characterised using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM), X-ray Diffraction analysis (XRD), Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR) spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, Ultra-Violet/Visible absorbance spectral analysis and photoluminescence spectroscopy. An interesting application of this system is the visual detection of Pb (II) ions in parts per billion levels, which is achieved through the aggregation of carbon nanoparticles. Scattering of light by the aggregated carbon dots allowed quantification of lead ions by simple turbidimetry method. Reports on carbon dot systems as visual Pb2+ sensor through turbidimetric route are nil in literature. This happens to be a highly significant observation when the lethal character of Pb (II) ions, a highly probable candidate in drinking water, is considered. Lead ions may enter drinking water when service pipes and other related accessories containing lead corrode in presence of acidic water/water with low mineral content [26]. According to the established standards, the maximum permissible amount of lead in drinking water is 15 μ g/L (15 parts per billion), as prescribed by U.S Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [26]. The limit of detection (LOD) of lead ions achieved by the present system through turbidimetric method is 14 ppb, which is highly remarkable in the light of the allowed concentration specified above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave assisted synthesis of carbon dots from table sugar (TS-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Table sugar was collected from the local market. Ammonia solution was obtained from Merck. Carbon dots were synthesised by the following procedure. Table sugar was heated at 150 °C for 3 min, which resulted in the formation of pale yellow slurry enriched with water molecules. Dilute ammonia solution was added, and the solution was subjected to microwave (M.W) irradiation for about 3 min at 120 °C. The solution was then cooled, filtered and subjected to freeze-drying to yield carbon dots.

HR-TEM image was recorded using Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin microscope. Sample for TEM analysis is prepared by dropping aqueous solution of carbon dots on copper grid coated with carbon. Elemental composition of the prepared system was determined through Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) by FEI ESEM QUANTA 200 3D instrument. Surface topography of the sample was studied using Atomic Force Microscope (AFM, NTEGRE (NT-MDT)) equipped with NOVA software in semi-contact mode by recording two-dimensional images. Sample for AFM analysis was prepared by placing a drop of suspension on the surface of freshly cleaved mica substrate X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was done using Rigaku MiniFlex-II diffractometer using Cu Kα1 radiation source. Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR) spectra were obtained using JASCO FTIR-4100 instrument using KBr disc method. Raman spectrum was obtained using LabRam HR-Horiba Jobinyvon Spectrometer using a Raman Microprobe with 532 nm Nd:YAG excitation source. LZC-4X photoreactor was used to study the ultra violet radiation (UV) assisted reactions. UV–vis absorbance was followed using JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer. A fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot was obtained with the aid of Perkin Elmer Fluorescence spectrometer LS 55, and LABTRONICS LT-33(Digital Turbidity Meter) was used for turbidimetric measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible mechanism of formation of TS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Possible mechanistic route to TS-CD formation can be obtained as follows. Caramelization of table sugar results in the decomposition of saccharose structure of table sugar into the mixture of component moieties, glucose and fructose [27,28,29]. The reducing sugar thus formed undergoes further reactions. It was observed that ammonia solution enhances the rate of this reaction [30]. The author also cited that “Ammonia caramel is formed in a Maillard-type reaction where carbonyl compounds react with amino groups or ammonia. The typical reaction between amino groups and reducing sugar is named after French chemist Louis Camille Maillard (1878–1936), who first described it in 1912 while attempting to reproduce biological protein [31]. The plausible pathway of the aforesaid reaction was first proposed by Hodges in 1953 [32]. According to him, the Maillard reaction results in plenty of products like N glycoside, ketosamine, Reductones and dehydro reductones, aroma compounds with unknown structural compositions etc. along with plenty of dehydration products like diacetyl, acetol or pyruvaldehyde. The buttery odor evolved during the carbonization process clearly points out the formation of diacetyl moiety, one of the major bi-products of the Maillard reaction, substantiating the fabrication route to TS-CD system through this reaction. Hence Maillard reaction is supposed to be the synthesis pathway to these dots, rather than simple caramelization of saccharose moiety. The products formed are expected to undergo a series of polymerization and condensation processes. Continuous exposure of M.W irradiation causes aromatization of the condensation/polymerization products, which subsequently leads to the production of aromatic clusters.These carbon nuclei formed then burst out when concentration of the formed clusters exceeds a critical super saturation level and results in the formation of carbon nano dots decorated with the functional groups. The schematic representation is provided in Scheme 1 .

The TEM image of carbon dots is shown in Fig. 1 (a), which clearly reveals the dispersed spherical nanoparticles. The particle size is estimated to be around 3.5 nm. The energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDAX) pattern (Fig. 1b) confirms the presence of carbon and oxygen in the prepared system. This data is able to substantiate the presence of carbon within the nanodot architecture. Morphology of the present system is further studied using atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis. Fig. 2 (c) represents the AFM image of a diluted solution of TS-CD system. Height profile (Fig. 1d) indicates that the average height of TS-CD system is around 3.5 nm, which is in good agreement with the TEM image (Fig. 1a). XRD pattern (Fig. 2a) clearly shows that the prepared TS-CD system possesses a rather amorphous carbonaceous core [33]. However, the pattern showed two distinct peaks; one at 2θ value 24° and the other at 44.5°. XRD peak observed at 2θ value 44.5° corresponds to inter planar spacing of the order 0.2 nm, which justifies the diffraction of X-rays from (101) planes of amorphous carbon core of graphitic nature. The peak at 24° corresponding to d spacing 0.37 nm signifies (002) graphitic planes which are definitely more separated than pure graphite indicating highly exfoliated graphitic nature of the material, which imparts more graphenic character to the system. FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2b) provides the following information regarding the functionalities of the system. The band at 1000 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of COC epoxy functional group. The asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate groups give rise to bands at 1648 cm−1 &1423 cm−1 respectively. The band at 3400 cm−1 can be attributed to OH stretching vibrations of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, and the shoulder band around 1700 cm−1 can be ascribed to the asymmetric carboxylate stretching, which also accounts for the moisture content that may be present in the synthesised system. Thus FTIR spectrum shows that the carbon core of TS-CD system is well decorated with carboxylate, hydroxyl and epoxy functionalities, carboxylate being the majority. Raman spectral profile of the prepared carbon dot system also illustrates the presence of a highly functionalized nano carbon framework of graphitic nature (Fig. 2c), as evident from the D band (1298 cm−1) corresponding to sp3 hybridized carbon moieties. G band observed at 1597 cm−1 account for sp2 carbons in the system. The ID/IG ratio provides a clear cut idea about the nature of carbon frame work inherent of the analyte [34]. The ID/IG ratio of the system is found to be 1.2, which indicates more defective carbon sites/plenty of surface functional groups [35]. FTIR spectral data showing oxygen rich functionalities substantiate this abovementioned conclusion.

Sharp intense band around 280 nm corresponding to π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 domains was noticed in the UV–vis spectrum of TS-CD system (Fig. 2d).This observation further substantiates the above mentioned probable mechanistic route of TS-CD formation, which includes aromatization process in one of the synthetic route. A tail in 300–370 nm region is also noticed, which is the inherent absorbance band that corresponds to the n-π* transitions of lone pair containing functionalities over the CD surface. Inset of Fig. 2(d) shows the digital photographs of CD solutions taken under day light and UV radiation (exc.400 nm) respectively. The intense green emission under UV irradiation confirms the fluorescence feature offered by the TS-CD system. This is again confirmed by the PL emission observed at 450 nm (Fig. 2e) when excited with radiation of wavelength 350 nm. The red shift in emission compared to absorption is due to the vibrational relaxation/vibrational cascade which is inherent of the fluorophores and is typical of photoluminescence [36]. The Quantum yield is found to be 2.5% (taking quinine sulphate as standard solution).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible detection of Pb2+ ion in parts per billion (ppb) levels
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the interference of other ions with the visible detection of Pb (II) ions, mixture of metal ions (Cd2+, Hg2+, Cu2+,Fe3+,K+, Na+,Ni2+, Co2+,Cr6+,Mn2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+) with and without the concerned ion was added in to the CD solution. Mixture of ions without Pb (II) ions failed in responding by aggregation. The moment when Pb (II) ions are added, the solution turns turbid, showing nil interference from the above mentioned ions (Fig. 4b). This commendable selectivity of the TS-CD system towards Pb (II) ions highlights its credibility as a prominent sensing gadget for the said ion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation of TS-CD system- verification through characterisation results
ce:section.ce:para: The reason behind visual detection of Pb2+ ions is suggested to be the aggregation of CDs in presence of Pb2+ ions, which is supported by various analytical results displayed in Fig. 5 (a–d). A comparison of TEM images before (Fig. 1a) and after (Fig. 5a) Pb2+ addition clearly indicate the aggregation process. The size of the aggregate becomes large enough to scatter light, which imparts turbidity to the solution. The EDAX data presented in Fig. 5(b) also underlines the incorporation of Pb (II) ions into the CD surface. Supporting evidence is also gathered from the photoluminescence spectra (Fig. 5c), which depicts the luminescent characteristic of carbon dot solution and the filtrate after the removal of aggregated particles. Figure portrays almost complete suppression of luminescence, revealing that carbon dots (reason behind the luminescence) have been completely removed. It can be noted that the XRD pattern exhibited by the aggregated portion (Fig. 5d) is very much similar to that of the prepared TS-CDs (Fig. 2a), except that the intensity was a little bit changed. This again confirms that the system is nothing but the aggregated carbon dots.

Reason behind the aggregation was pointed to be the loss of stability of individual carbon dot with the addition of Pb (II) ions. Systematic analysis of FTIR spectrum and its exclusive hydrophilicity point out that the excess carboxylate groups present over the carbon dot surface accounts for this negative charge of the TS-CD system. It is highly probable that the added Pb2+ions nullify the negative charge of the system, which is achieved by the interaction of the metal ion and the carboxylate functionalities present over the TS-CD surface. Literature also cites the highly selective extra ordinary affinity of Pb (II) ions towards carboxylate groups [37]. This suggestion is strongly backed by the reduced intensity of CO stretching bands of carboxylate groups (seen around 1560 and 1410 cm−1, signifying asymmetric and symmetric stretching), especially the symmetric CO stretching of the aggregated CDs in the FTIR spectra (Fig. 6 ). The involvement of carboxylate groups in the aggregation process is quite clear from this observation. It can be proposed that the major portion of the added lead ions interacts with the carboxylate functionalities present over the carbon core due to its anomalous affinity towards carboxylate ions. The XRD pattern of TS-CD system has clearly illustrated well separated graphitic units tending to graphenic nature. Previous works have emphasized higher aggregation of graphenic units in presence of Pb (II) ions [38,39]. Besides, Senthil Kumar and group have reported recently that CDs with less surface passivation and large carbon core (as in the present case) are highly susceptible for aggregation in presence of an external agent [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative sensing of Pb2+ ion –Turbidimetric analysis
ce:section.ce:para: As the presence of Pb2+ is displayed by turbidity, turbidimetry was used for quantifying Pb (II) ions. It can be seen that administration of increasing amount of Pb (II) ions in carbon dot solution progressively increased the turbidity, and the variation was perfectly linear (R2 = 0.996) with the concentration of added Pb (II) ions (Fig. 7 ). As per the data, the limiting amount (as achieved via 3σ/slope method) was 14 ppb, an observation highly noteworthy in view of the fact that permissible amount of lead in drinking water is 15 ppb (Lead and copper rule by U.S Environment Protection Agency (EPA) published in 1991) [26]. The permissible amount of Pb(II) ion for oral administration, parenteral exposure and inhalation exposure as per the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and International Conference on Harmonization (ICH) guideline is 0.1 ppm and 0.5 ppm respectively [41,42]. It is quite clear that our system excels in its efficiency in terms of both these guidelines.

The method was both accurate and reliable, suggesting that the present sensor has great potential for application in fast, sensitive and low-cost detection of Pb2+ in water. The fact that a simple turbidimeter is enough to achieve such a low LOD further enhances the significance of this work. Time dependence of the aggregation process was monitored using 14 ppb of Pb (II) solution. From the result presented in Fig. 8 , it is understood that except the concentration of analyte ions, no other factor interferes in the aggregation process.

Several standard reports exist for the determination of Pb2+ ions at lower thresholds. Fluorimetric procedures based on various inorganic and organic sensors have been reported extensively in the literature [43–52]. Colorimetric procedures based on gold nanoparticles and lead-stabilized G-quartet formation also were brought to light by some research groups [53]. Naked eye detection of lead ions in ppb levels by monitoring the colour change of the solution was also coined by them. A much lower detection limit was reported by Chai and co-workers using glutathione functionalized gold nanoparticles by colorimetric route [54]. In recent years, electrochemical sensors and analysis methods have been developed for heavy metal detection, due to their advantages of small size, high sensitivity, low cost and easy operation [55–58]. Detection limit attained by them were in ppm/ppb levels. However, most of the solid electrodes have drawbacks such as cross-contamination, electrode surface poisoning and other issues that affect the analysis results. Reports presenting other significant results are tabulated in Table 1 . Among this group, a major fraction is invariably marked by toxic chemicals in synthesis part and demanded tedious and expensive methodologies [59–62]. In some other cases Pb (II) sensing was significantly interfered in the presence of co-cations [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of TS-CDs sensing efficiency using real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Viability of the proposed carbon dot system as an efficient layman’s tool for the detection of Pb (II) ions is monitored by analysing three real water samples collected from local premises. Samples collected from Tap water (University of calicut), well water (Calicut) and lake water (Calicut) (labelled as sample A,B and C respectively) are analysed to this effect. All the three samples were spiked with known concentration of Pb (II) ions. Blank samples and the spiked samples were subjected to Pb (II) detection analysis with the aid of the prepared sensor. The data related to this area is provided in Table 2 .

The prepared TS-CDs were able to exhibit excellent sensing efficiency towards all the analyzed samples. Spiked recoveries corresponding to all the spiked concentrations (20, 25 and 30 μ g/L) of the samples A, B and C were in the range of 98–99.7%. It is quite noteworthy that the error percentages associated with the spiked recovery calculation of real water samples fall ∼ 0.25. These commendable spiked recovery values promise the practical applicability of the present sensing gadget as a good alternative for ordinary Pb (II) sensing units. All the aforesaid features project the present TS-CD based sensor as a beacon sensing gadget for the extremely toxic Pb (II) ions.

Simple microwave irradiation of ammoniated table sugar slurry was carried out for the fabrication of luminescent carbon dots. Even though the literature is rich with a good quantum of carbon dot based Pb (II) sensors with commendable features, visual sensing of this lethal ion via facile tactics is never reported. Naked eye detection of a toxic metal ion in ppb levels with extreme selectivity is considered to be a laudable feature for a sensing gadget. It is noteworthy that a simple turbidimeter is able to quantify Pb (II) ions. The extreme fleetness and minimalism associated with this afore said quantification strategy is strong enough to escalate the merit of the prepared carbon dot solution as an eminent visual sensor. The LOD achieved is 14 ppb, with a linear range of turbidity at ppb level Pb2+ concentration, with regression coefficient of 0.99. Reliability of the system is validated by analysing real water samples collected from local premises and good ranges of spiked recoveries were attained. As commercially available table sugar is serving as the starting material, the present work can be considered as an economic strategy for naked eye detection of analytes in an efficient manner.
The authors declare that they have no conflicts of interest concerning this article.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Ansi V.A gratefully acknowledges the financial assistance received from the Government of Kerala, and the Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut for the facilities provided.

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots chemosensor for selective turn-on sensing of Zn2+ and turn-off sensing of Pb2+ in aqueous medium and zebra fish eggs
New J. Chem.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: V.A. Ansi had her Post Graduation and M.Phil. Degree from University of Calicut, Kerala, India. Presently she is pursuing research for Ph.D. in the Department of Chemistry of University of Calicut under the corresponding author. Her research area is Carbon nanodots.

N.K. Renuka is presently Associate Professor in the Department of Chemistry of University of Calicut, Kerala, India. She had her Post Graduation from University of Calicut, and M. Phil. and Ph.D. Degrees from Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kerala. Her current research interests are Cabon nano materials and sensors.
